Package Review: Broader Validation Pass

Source: R2.md

Date: 2026-03-21

Validation Performed

Test suite

  • Running testthat::test_dir("tests/testthat") directly is not meaningful for this package because the tests assume the package namespace is already loaded.
  • Running the tests in the expected mode with pkgload::load_all(".", quiet = TRUE); testthat::test_dir("tests/testthat") completed successfully.

Package check

  • R CMD check --no-manual --as-cran . stopped early in this environment with a metadata error claiming Author and Maintainer were missing from DESCRIPTION.
  • The package uses Authors@R, so this looks more like an environment or tooling mismatch than a confirmed package defect.
  • I am not treating that R CMD check output as a verified package bug.

Additional Findings

1. High: char.sep is internally inconsistent and can break grouping

Files: - /Users/ellessenne/R-dev/rsimsum/R/utils.R - /Users/ellessenne/R-dev/rsimsum/R/simsum.R - /Users/ellessenne/R-dev/rsimsum/R/multisimsum.R

Details: - The package documents char.sep as a configurable separator used when splitting grouped data. - However, .validate_levels() uses grepl(char, levels(...)), so the separator is treated as a regular expression rather than a literal string. - This means regex metacharacters such as | are effectively unusable. - Separately, .split_by() still hardcodes sep = "~", so the actual split logic ignores the configured separator.

Validation: - With control = list(char.sep = "|"), simsum() failed validation even though no pipe character was present in the grouping levels. - With control = list(char.sep = "_") and grouping levels containing ~, simsum() reached a runtime failure during summary assembly instead of keeping groups separate.

Why tests miss it: - Current tests only validate .validate_levels() with a few simple literal characters. - I did not find tests that exercise char.sep through simsum() or multisimsum().

2. Medium: summary() accepts ci_level = 0 and ci_level = 1

Files: - /Users/ellessenne/R-dev/rsimsum/R/summary.simsum.R - /Users/ellessenne/R-dev/rsimsum/R/summary.multisimsum.R

Details: - Both summary methods allow boundary values because ci_level is checked with lower = 0 and upper = 1. - These endpoints produce nonsensical confidence intervals.

Validation: - summary(x, ci_level = 1) produced intervals with lower = -Inf and upper = Inf. - summary(x, ci_level = 0) produced zero-width intervals equal to the point estimate.

Why tests miss it: - The tests cover values outside the interval, such as negative numbers or values greater than 1. - They do not check boundary values exactly equal to 0 or 1.

3. Medium: invalid power_df values are accepted and can silently force power = 0

Files: - /Users/ellessenne/R-dev/rsimsum/R/simsum.R - /Users/ellessenne/R-dev/rsimsum/R/performance.R

Details: - control$power_df is only checked for being numeric. - Nonpositive values are accepted even though they are not valid degrees of freedom for qt(). - The resulting NaN critical value propagates into the power calculation and ends up reported as 0.

Validation: - With power_df = 0, power was reported as 0. - With power_df = -1, power was also reported as 0. - The underlying call to qt() emits NaN warnings for such inputs.

Why tests miss it: - Existing tests only verify that positive values of power_df change the result. - There are no tests for invalid or nonpositive degrees of freedom.

Overall Result

  • The package test suite passes in the expected execution mode.
  • The broader validation pass did not reveal additional failing shipped tests.
  • It did expose three additional edge-case defects or robustness gaps:
    1. broken char.sep handling
    2. invalid boundary handling for ci_level
    3. silent acceptance of invalid power_df

Suggested Follow-up

  1. Treat char.sep as a literal string in validation and use it consistently in splitting logic.
  2. Restrict ci_level to the open interval (0, 1).
  3. Require power_df to be strictly positive when supplied.
  4. Add regression tests for all three cases.

On this page

Developed by Alessandro Gasparini, Ian R. White.
